speaker: interviewee
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: Should I take these away?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. There's nothing--

speaker: interviewee
text: They're not going to get heat?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to munch on them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know, but they're not going to get heat, are they?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, take them away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Are they going to get heat?

speaker: interviewer
text: Put them right here close to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Survivors of the Shoah interview October 19, 1994. Survivor Brigitte Medvin. Interviewer Dana Schwartz in the city of Los Angeles. The interview will be conducted in English. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Brigitte, tell me your name and tell me a little bit about your background and your childhood.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My name is Brigitte Medvin, and I was born in Berlin, Germany in 1933. So now you know how old I am. [LAUGHS] My father was Polish, and my mother was German. My father had come from Poland, from a little village, with his three brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was called [NON-ENGLISH]. And my grandparents, whom I never knew, I only have a picture of my grandmother. My fath-- my grandfather, from what I understand, was a shamas in the local shul. And my-- the three brothers immigrated to Germany. And my two uncles started a Coke manufacturing company. And my father, who was the rebel in the family, came to Berlin to study music and become a musician, and which he did. And my mother was born in Berlin of parents from Berlin. They were assimilated Jews. You know, the Jews were-- in Berlin, they were not very religious. And my grandfather was a tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know these people's names? The-- the grandparent's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my grandmother's name was Anna, on my mother's side. And my grandfather on my mother's side was Max. But I don't remember the name of my grandparents on my father's side. I really can't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your last name was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Fuhrman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fuhrman.

speaker: interviewee
text: At first it was F-U-H-R-M-A-N when I was born, and then later on, as a professional name, towards, I think the end of the war and after the war, my father changed it to F-U-R-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your mother's maiden?

speaker: interviewee
text: Heuermann. H-E-U-E-R-M-A-N-N. Anyway, my grandfather on my mother's side was a tailor. And my parents met when my-- this is what I hear-- when my father wanted to-- somebody suggested my grandfather that he go to him to have tailoring done. My father needed suits for his business, for his music, for his appearances and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's where he met my mother. And they fell in love. And of course, they were married, and I was born. And my first recollection of those years was when I think-- I think I was 2 or 3 years old. I was-- how long can you-- how far can you remember back? I don't know. The only-- the first recollection of my mother was I was in a bedroom with her, and the paramedics carried her out of the bedroom. And I asked my grandmother and my aunt, I said, what's wrong? What's wrong? Oh Mom, your mommy is very ill.

speaker: interviewee
text: It turned out that my mother became a brittle diabetic, and she was very, very ill for quite awhile. My father, who had been away on-- my father traveled with his orchestra on engagements all over Europe-- had been away, quickly came back. And we were reunited, and then my mother got better. And we lived together for a very short time, which I remember. And then Hitler came to power. And my father-- I don't know if you remember, if you saw the movie the my father, as I said before, was the rebel, and he was very contemporary and very modern. And in his musical studies, he learned how to play American jazz. And he loved American jazz. So this was his forte at the time. And he traveled with an orchestra that was like the Stan Kenton or Glenn Miller of the times. And they would perform everywhere, all over Europe. And one time, several times my father was given a warning that if he continued to play American music, or if he continued to play music, that he would have to pay the consequences. And I remember my dad telling me that because he was a professional, and because he, you know, had the status that he had, that they would let him leave the country. And my father then decided, being an artist and not a very smart man, not a businessman, not intelligent in that way, he decided that we would go back to Poland, where his home was, because he had film commitments in Warsaw. And unfortunately, my father left first. And I remember my mother and I followed soon thereafter. And we all ended up in Warsaw. And I remember, it was one of the most beautiful times. We lived in this magnificent apartment. We lived in this apartment building that had a huge courtyard with beautiful trees. And I would look out the window across to the other building. And every day-- I inherited my father's talents. And every day, I would spend hours in front of the mirror, playing different characters and different personalities. I wanted to be an actress.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my parents lived there. And we lived there for awhile. And one day, my father came home and he said, you're coming with me tomorrow. I have an audition for you for a film. So we've gotten this far, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just backtracking a little bit, what do you remember about the kind of home you lived in before the war, the kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know we lived in--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the degree of religious observance?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was none.

speaker: interviewer
text: None.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was very-- there was none. Before the war, there was very little. We-- we lived in a beautiful apartment. And I remember, my mother would always take me for tea on the [INAUDIBLE]. And she would always be very proud of me. You know, we'd stroll up and down, you know, the [INAUDIBLE], and we'd have afternoon tea. And those were the recollections that I have of that time. And it wasn't-- as I said before, my father was not religious. My mother was not religious. And we had very little religious input except for some of the friends that we had, you know, we would get together for holidays and with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew you were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, I-- well, I-- I don't know if I-- I knew I that I was Jewish. But then later on, I was totally told not to be. I mean, I was, let's see, at five, six years old, yes, I knew I was Jewish. Of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So your father-- I-- I interrupted you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father came and told you that-- that you had a movie role.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right. And he took me to the audition. And I remember it very well. There were a lot of young girls. It was a part for a six-year-old child that was to be the controversy between a divorcing couple or a separating couple. And I-- I remember it so well because I had to play a scene where I was supposed to run-- my father was ill in the hospital, and I was to run into his arms and hug him and visit with him. And every time I would rush to the bed and have to throw my arms around him, I would stop dead in my tracks because I hated the man who played my father. And I-- nothing happened. And we went home. And my father was furious. And he said, I can't believe that you're doing this. You're talented. You know what you're doing. And he said, they're giving you one more chance. We're going back tomorrow. And for your incentive, if you do this scene, I have a big surprise for you. Anyway, we went back the next day, and I finally ended up doing the scene. And I remember-- I mean, in those days-- this is 1939-- there was the most beautiful toy stove, a metal toy stove filled with dishes and flatware and all kinds of pots and pans. I mean, it was unheard of in those days. And that was my gift. And I did the film. And then shortly thereafter-- I remember the day so vividly-- we were told that Hitler was advancing. And everybody-- I remember my mother taping up all the windows with thick tapes because if the bombs exploded that the glass wouldn't crash on you. And I was in my same bedroom where I always used to look myself in the mirror and play my different parts, and I opened up the window, and I looked outside the window, and there suddenly was this tremendous explosion. A bomb had dropped in the courtyard between the two buildings, the one I told you about that I saw on the other side. A bomb had dropped and exploded. Anyway, we rushed to the basement, to the shelter. But unfortunately, the shelter was on the other side, in the other building.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while the bombs were falling all around us, we ran into that shelter across the lawn. And there were people that were injured and people bleeding. Anyway, my mother and I ended up in the shelter, what I think must have been days and weeks. I think it was a month or so. I don't know how long the war was. I can't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was in and out because I think he was helping the Polish army. And we were in that shelter so long that we ended up not having food. The only thing that we had to eat was bread filled with worms and prunes, and dried up prunes. And I remember, I had lice in my head. And my mother used to pick the lice. She used to go like this and pick the lice off my head. And one day she said, I'm sorry. I can't stand this anymore. You and I are going to go to the apartment, and I have to wash your hair. And I was so scared because, again, we had to go across the courtyard to the other building. And she took me up there, and she washed my hair. And I couldn't wait to get back into the shelter. And during that time, I remember they were bringing people in, wounded soldiers. And they were bloody, and they were-- I mean, some of them were dead, some of them were dying. And my mother and a lot of the older women were trying to help the nurses. And several times, some people went outside with knives and took-- there were horses, because in that war, there was still a horse brigade. There were horses lying on the street. And people would go out and cut meat from those dead horses and bring it in, and we would cook the horse meat so that we would have something to eat. And then the war ended. And my parents and I, I remember going back to the apartment. And it was a very short time thereafter that my grandparents on my mother's side stayed in Berlin, and they were helped by Gentile friends of theirs. And they were also helped through my uncle. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. You said the war ended. You mean that part of the war stopped.

speaker: interviewee
text: That part of the war. Right. That part of the war. And Hitler was in control.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was very dangerous for us to be together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my parents sent me back to Berlin. As Jews, they wanted me out of the way. I mean, this was in Warsaw, when Hitler overran Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my parents wanted me out of the way, so they sent me back to Berlin for my grandmother to take care of me. And shortly thereafter, my parents were brought in to the Warsaw ghetto. They were rounded up, like so many other people, and they ended up in the ghetto for about six or eight months. But my father, because of his status as-- as a musician and a well-known person in that-- in that realm, and also, fortunately, there was money and jewelry. We-- in those days, they still had Polish and German guards. And we were able to buy your way out of the ghetto. And that's while I was-- During that time, I was in Germany. And my grandmother was trying to take care of me. And she tried to even get me into the local school. I know we lived on a street called [INAUDIBLE] in Potsdam. And she enrolled me in the local school at the age of six or seven, I guess. Because I know it was after-- yeah, '40, '41, something like that. I can't think of the timing now. But anyway, she tried to enroll me in school, which she did. And I remember the first day going to school. And when you go to school in Germany, you carry a sweet bag. It's called a tute. And it's-- it's like a cone, like a huge cone. And the more money you have and-- and-- and the more generous your parents are, the bigger is the cone. And it's filled with goodies and gifts and-- and sweets and all of that. And I remember going to school very proudly. And I spent--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you carry this every day or just at the beginning?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. This is the first day at school. It's like a present that you get for the first day of school. And I lived with my grandmother in those days. And I remember my aunt and uncle, my aunt, Dolly, and my uncle, Bruno. In the meantime, I found out that one of the uncles who was in Germany immigrated to London, England. He emigrated to England. My Uncle Bruno and my Aunt Dolly did not-- she did not want to leave Berlin. She believed that they would be saved, that nothing would happen to them. And they ended up as one of the last Jews in Berlin, the people who walked around with the yellow star and the people who tried to hide till the very end. And my aunt tells me this story that one of the friends that my uncle had, they were doing some black market things in order to keep themselves alive because they had to live quietly, and they had to live in hiding. One day, my uncle went out, and he-- the very friend that he used to work with betrayed him and turned him in. And my uncle ended up in Auschwitz in a concentration camp. And my aunt, after she found out what happened to him, had to change apartments or had to change where she was living. And she tells me the story that she finally got a room with a woman, a Gentile woman, who promised her that she would take care of her and everything, not knowing what her background was. And my aunt said that she used to have nightmares, and she used to scream. And she must-- she said, I must have come out with who I am and what happened to Bruno in my dreams because the next thing I knew, there was a knock on the door, and they turned me in. And both my aunt and uncle ended up in Auschwitz. And my uncle was killed, but my aunt luckily survived. And she too came to this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. When I was-- as I said, I came back to my grandmother. I lived with my grandmother. And I remember, I started telling you my aunt and uncle came with the yellow stars. And I saw the stars, and my grandmother-- and my grandmother explained to me, and she said, you see, that's why I don't want you telling anybody who you are. She always told me, when people ask, where are your parents, tell them that your mother is somewhere on vacation. And she told me someplace where I should tell where she went and that my father is in the army. My father is not here. And after awhile, things got very, very bad. The principal of the school came to my grandmother and told her I cannot keep her in school anymore because she will not be safe here. She will have to leave, and you will have to take her. And I remember that my grandmother took me and sent me away for awhile to a friend of hers who lived way out in the country somewhere. It was someplace where she felt they would not come after me or anything like that. In the meantime, time went by, and my parents left the ghetto. And when they-- they left the ghetto under assumed names. Their name was Stravinsky. And I guess my father took that name because of his music and his violin. [CHUCKLES] He figured that was a safe name. I don't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they come out?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were-- as I said, they bought their way out of the ghetto. They had money. My mother had jewelery. And they were able to buy their way out of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whom did they pay to get out of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The actual people, I don't know. But these were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Germans or Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. These were-- I told you. At-- at the beginning, there were Polish and German guards. And these were the Polish guards. These were people that my father knew. And through connections, these people, I guess, got to my father, got to them somebody that he knew from the outside, and they were able to be bribed. They were bribed. And quite a few people got out that way in the beginning. And as my mother tells the story, they came out of the ghetto, and they took an apartment, and they lived under an assumed name. But not for very long were they safe. Because the very people who took the bribe to get my parents out would come back again and again for more money. And my father finally decided that they had to separate. And my father went into the villages outside of Warsaw. And my mother said that he played his fiddle there in the local church in order to survive. And she would go visit him on weekends, try to sneak out and visit him. And one time, she came back, and there were people in her apartment. And they started beating her and hitting her. And my mother ended up with a big scar on her lip. They wanted to know where my father was. They said that they would turn her in if she didn't tell them where my father was. And it was just that way. I mean, then-- I can't remember. I was-- I came-- right after that, I came back. My father had already gone into the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came back from Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came back from Germany because my grandmother told my mother that she could not safely keep me anymore. And so my grandmother shipped me back. Who-- I think there were people who-- who must have take-- taken me up and back because I remember going on the train, but I don't remember the people that were with me on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were about eight then?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's very strange.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in, let's see, 1942. Does that sound right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Nine?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was like nine. Yeah. Anyway, maybe she put me on the train, and somebody waited for me on the other end. I don't remember. All I know, I traveled the train several times up and back between Warsaw and Berlin. And then when I came back, and my father was gone, and my mother was very, very desperate, she needed-- oh, and we did have friends in Warsaw. I remember my mother and I living with friends in a very, very large apartment. There were many bedrooms. And they let us stay with them in one of their bedrooms. And we lived with them. But my mother needed a job. And she needed to work. And my mother ended up working for a German officers club as a waitress.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a Pole.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a Pole, as a waitress. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's how we lived there, and that's how we survived for awhile. I remember, those were horrible times because the underground in Warsaw and all over Poland was constantly working. And there was-- just like there is in Israel today, there were bombings, explosions on the streets. Some of the Gestapo houses were sabotaged. German soldiers walking down the street-- all of a sudden, you're walking down the street, and then you hear gunfire, and a couple of German soldiers were killed on the street. And it was very dangerous and not very safe to be. And that's-- I remember my life with my mother and myself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And towards the end of that, I think I'm missing-- I think maybe I went back again to my grandmother's for awhile. Because I remember I went to my grandmother, and she sent me off on somebody's farm. I lived on a farm for an awful long time. And I remember riding big horses bareback. And I remember walking the fields and-- and playing games with children on this farm, that I lived there for quite awhile.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still as a Pole.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no. That was in Germany. When-- when I was in Germany, I was always a German. When I was in Poland, I was the Stravinsky daughter. So it was that kind of a thing. I was-- I don't think, when I was in Germany during those times, I don't think I had an identity, except for the first time when I went to school. I was registered. I was in school. But after that, I was just living with different people in different places in order not to be--

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did the war impact you? How-- how was it for you during the war? What made it feel scary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was-- I mean, I was constantly, constantly scared. I remember I lived through the air raids in Berlin, some of them. And my grandparents lived in an apartment building. I think there were four stories or five stories. And during the war, the elevators were not working. There was an elevator, and then there were the spiral staircases all the way up. And I remember, we lived on the top. And during those air raids, I would always go to sleep with all my clothes on, with my shoes on, with my clothes on. And I remember one time-- sometimes the sirens went off, and there wasn't time enough to get to the shelters. So one time I remember the sirens went off, and I tripped on the top stairwell, and I spiraled all the way down until I got to the next floor, but luckily, I wasn't hurt. And I was running. And sometimes, because the shelter was not in our building, we had to walk outside about a half a block to the shelter in the next building. Sometimes you couldn't get out because the flak had started to fall from the bombs, and you could hear it. It was like-- like huge stones falling on the sidewalk. And it was too dangerous. And then you had to wait until the flak stopped falling, and then you would run into the shelters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what was happening in the ghetto in-- in Warsaw? Did you have any idea what was happening to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I knew that bad things were happening because of my own situation. I mean, I was old enough now to realize who I was and what I was. And I was always afraid to trip up and say the wrong things. And I knew bad things were happening. I knew a lot of people were being killed. But I was a young child. I was what, 11 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in-- I remember being in Warsaw with my mother. This is now in 1944, I think. That's when there was the big uprising. I was alone with my mother at that time in Warsaw. My mother, because of her work with the Germans, came home one day. We had-- she had a girlfriend living with us. We lived in an apartment. I remember the street name was [INAUDIBLE]. I'm sure it's still there today. Anyhow, my mother came home one day, and she said, there is going to be big fighting in the city. And we were told that if we wanted to, it was OK. [BACKGROUND NOISE] We should pack our things and leave the city because it would not be safe for us to-- to stay.
